title: Chapter 27: 90 Seconds
content: I wake up and for exactly ninety seconds, I don't remember.

The ceiling is there. The light is gray. My hand moves to the nightstand by muscle memory, fingers finding the phone screen. I tap. The display wakes. The Mephisto icon pulses once — that red-black gradient, the M with the serifs that look like horns — and it comes back.
Not as a feeling. As a fact.
I have no feelings.

The ninety seconds before the fact loads are the worst part. Not because they hurt — nothing hurts anymore — but because for ninety seconds I was a person who *might* have felt something. Some hypothetical version of Chloe Hart, opening her eyes in the morning, maybe dreading the day, maybe craving coffee, maybe reaching across the sheets for the warmth of another body. A person for whom "might" was still on the table.
Now I'm a person who knows it isn't.
Nine zero. I count them. Exactly ninety. Every morning the same interval. I don't know why yet. I just know the number.
I get up.

The shower runs cold because I don't adjust the handle. The water hits my shoulders and registers as: temperature differential, approximately 12 degrees below skin surface. My nerve endings report the data. Nothing converts it to discomfort. I stand under the cold and it's just — wet. Cold wet. Data.

I towel off. I dress. Jeans, black hoodie, clean socks. The fabric touches skin and each point of contact is logged and filed and nothing means anything. The smell of the apartment reaches me — old wood, coffee residue, the faint chemical tang of Leo's dry-erase markers — and my olfactory system catalogs it all as environmental data. Not home. Not comfort. Just inputs.

I make coffee. Two scoops into the filter, water to the line, press the button. The machine gurgles. I pour it into a mug that says VILLAGER ENERGY on it. I take a sip. It tastes like coffee. Bitter, roasted, faintly acidic. My tongue identifies each flavor note and routes it nowhere. I swallow.

Leo is at the kitchen counter. His laptop is open, the Hollowed Files spread across the screen in a cascade of annotated columns. He's been up all night — I can tell by the red in his eyes, the way his hair defies gravity on one side, the three empty mugs lined up like evidence. He's highlighting passages, cross-referencing account IDs with doxxed user profiles, building a map of Mephisto's extraction architecture from the inside out.
I sit across from him. I watch him work.

He's fascinating the way a specimen is fascinating. Efficient. Methodical. His fingers move across the keyboard in precise bursts, then pause while he reads, then move again. The tendons in his hands shift under the skin. His jaw tightens when he finds something. He looks tired.
I observe this. I note it. I do not feel concern.
"You should sleep," I say.
He looks up. His eyes are bloodshot, the whites mapped with red threads. "You said that yesterday."
"Did I?"

I don't remember saying it. But the words came out of my mouth in the right cadence, the right tone, so they must have been there before. Muscle memory. My voice knows the shape of caring even if I can't feel the thing itself.
Leo holds my gaze for a moment. He's looking for something in my face. I don't know what he expects to find. Whatever it is, he doesn't find it, because he turns back to his screen.
"Almost done with the cross-ref," he says. "Then I'll crash."
I drink my coffee. It's cooling. The temperature drop is data. The mug is warm in my hands. Tactile input. Logged and discarded.
My phone chimes. The sound is sharp, a single tone — Evelyn's ringtone, the default I never bothered to change. I answer.
"The TRO was denied."
Evelyn's voice is clipped, efficient. She's reading from a prepared statement, probably her own notes. She does that — delivers legal updates like bench rulings, no filler.

"Judge found the harm speculative. No precedent for emotional data extraction as a form of irreparable injury. The existing case law covers psychological harm from defamation, harassment, intentional infliction of emotional distress — but nothing about a corporation literally removing your capacity to feel. It's too novel. The court doesn't know how to categorize it, so it defaults to 'not proven.'"
I process this. The TRO was a long shot. I knew that. Evelyn knew that. The denial is a data point, not a disaster.

"But the case itself proceeds," she continues. "The hearing is in seventy-one hours. And here's the thing — the denial of the TRO is actually useful. It establishes that the court considered the question and found it *novel*. Which means the hearing will get more scrutiny. A motion that gets denied because the issue is unprecedented is a motion that puts the issue on the radar. Judges love making precedent. The first judge to rule on emotional data extraction as a compensable injury? That's a career-making opinion."
She pauses. The line is quiet except for the faint hiss of the connection.
Then: "Chloe, how are you?"

The question lands like a foreign object. It's the first time Evelyn has asked me a personal question in the entire legal proceedings. Not "how's your emotional state relevant to the case" or "can you characterize the harm for the record." Just — how are you.
I answer honestly.
"I don't know how to answer that."
Silence. Then Evelyn, after a beat: "That's the best answer you could give."
Another pause. Longer this time.

"It proves the harm. Remember that for your testimony. When they ask how you're doing, say exactly what you said to me. 'I don't know how to answer that.' A person who can feel knows how they feel. A person who can't feel — can't. It's self-proving. Don't let them coach you into something neater."
She hangs up. No goodbye. That's Evelyn.

I set the phone down. The Evelyn-shaped input is filed. The TRO denial: expected. The hearing timeline: relevant. The testimony strategy: useful. The personal question: anomalous. I don't know what to do with it, so I set it aside.
"Leo."
He doesn't look up. "Yeah."
"She asked how I was."
"Who?"
"Evelyn."

His fingers stop. He looks up. His expression does something complicated — surprise, then something softer, then nothing, like he's controlling it. Which he is. For me. Because my inability to process emotional input doesn't mean he gets to stop having it. He just can't show it around me without it becoming noise.
"What did you say?"
"I don't know how to answer that."
He almost smiles. Almost. "That's perfect."
"I know. She told me."
He turns back to his screen. Then stops. Swivels his chair to face me fully.
"I found something."

His voice has changed. The tiredness is still there but there's something underneath it — a frequency I recognize from before. When Leo finds a pattern, his whole body shifts. His shoulders pull back. His eyes focus. He's like a dog on a scent.
"Show me."

He turns the laptop toward me. The Hollowed Files. He's been mapping them — not just reading, but graphing. Each hollowed creator's emotional baseline over time, plotted like seismograph readings. Most of the charts look the same: a jagged skyline of normal emotional variation, then a sudden drop to flatline. Love, trust, empathy, grief — all zeroed out. The charts look like cities after a bombing.
But Leo's highlighted something different. A different kind of data.
"Look at the timestamps," he says. "Not the flatlines. The *resets*."
I look. Each creator's chart has a daily timestamp — midnight, or whenever the Mephisto app syncs with the server. A reset point where the flatline is enforced again.

"There's a gap," Leo says. "Every single one. Right after the consciousness reset — that's sleep-to-wake, the brain coming back online — there's a ninety-second interval where the enforcement hasn't loaded yet."

I stare at the charts. He's right. In each one, there's a tiny blip at the beginning of each day. Ninety seconds where the flatline isn't flat. Where the line flickers upward — not to normal, but to something. A residue. A ghost signal.
"It's like a boot sequence," he says. "The system has to reinitialize the emotional flatline every time consciousness resets. For ninety seconds, the original emotional architecture is still in RAM."
"RAM," I repeat.

"Random access memory. The stuff your brain is actively using. The deductions — the emotional removal — they're stored in long-term memory. Enforced by the app's neural interface. But when you first wake up, the app hasn't synced yet. It takes about ninety seconds for the system to reload the enforcement protocol into your active consciousness. During that window—"
"Are you saying there's a window?"
My voice is flat. I'm not asking with hope. I'm asking for clarification.

Leo meets my eyes. "I'm saying there's a *glitch*. During those ninety seconds, you're technically still capable of feeling. The system just overwrites it every morning. Like a computer overwriting temporary files on startup. The original data is there. It just gets replaced before you can access it."

The coffee mug is still in my hands. I look at it. VILLAGER ENERGY. The font is pixelated, old-school. I bought this mug at a convention in 2023. I liked the design. That's a fact about a person who existed. I am that person. The person who liked the design. I just can't access the liking anymore.
But if Leo is right — if the liking is still there, just overwritten — then it's not gone. It's archived. Compressed. Locked behind a protocol that runs every morning at boot.
"If you could stop the overwrite," I say.

"Then the original emotions would remain in place." He's leaning forward now, talking fast. "The deductions would still be recorded in the Mephisto system — they'd still show up on the backend as 'processed' — but they wouldn't be *enforced*. The lock would be gone. It's like—"
"DRM," I say.
He blinks. "What?"

"DRM. Digital rights management. The music industry used to lock songs so you could only play them on authorized devices. But the song itself — the file — was still there. The lock was just a protocol. Remove the protocol, and the file plays anywhere."
Leo stares at me. "That's... exactly right. Yeah. The file is still there. The lock is gone."
I set the mug down. The ceramic clicks against the counter. The sound is precise, measurable. 440 hertz, approximately. A concert A. I file it.
"Can you write it?"
The question isn't emotional. It's strategic. Can you build the tool? Yes or no. Binary.
Leo's jaw works. His eyes drop to the laptop, then back to me.

"I can write it. The code isn't the hard part. It's a blocking script — intercept the Mephisto sync signal during the boot window, prevent it from overwriting the emotional baseline. Like a firewall rule. Reject the payload before it loads."
"But."

"But I need access to the gateway. The Mephisto neural interface operates through their servers. I'd need to deploy the script on *their* end, or at least at the network layer between their servers and the app's local instance. The same gateway that—" He stops.
The same gateway that would kill him. The gateway that nearly fried Leo's nervous system the last time he got close to Mephisto's infrastructure. We both know this. Neither of us says it.
"Then we find another way in," I say.

I pick up my phone. The Mephisto icon pulses on the home screen — red-black, horned M, patient. It's always there. It's always waiting. It's the most patient thing in my life. More patient than Evelyn. More patient than the legal system. More patient than Leo, who is patient in a way that registers as data and nothing else.

I tap the icon. Not to open the app — I open the contract instead. All one hundred pages of it. The Terms of Service I agreed to when I was twenty-three and stupid and thought I was signing up for a creator network, not a soul extraction service.

I've read most of it by now. The arbitration clause. The data usage section. The indemnification paragraph that basically says Mephisto isn't responsible for anything, ever, including the heat death of the universe. But I haven't read *all* of it. Nobody reads all of it. That's the point.

I scroll. Past the sections I've memorized. Past the sections Evelyn has annotated with red-pen fury in the margins of her printed copy. Past the definitions, the warranties, the limitations of liability.
Page seventy-three.
Section 44(b).

*"Creator may request an audit of all personal data held by Mephisto MCN by submitting a formal request through the application's settings menu. Mephisto MCN shall provide access to such data within thirty (30) business days, including but not limited to: all stored personal information, behavioral analytics, emotional baseline recordings, and derivative data products derived from Creator's content and engagement metrics."*
I read it again.

An audit. A *legal right* to access the system. Not a hack. Not a breach. A clause built into the contract — probably inserted by some Mephisto lawyer who never imagined a hollowed creator would actually use it. They put it there to comply with data access regulations, the same way every tech company includes a "you can request your data" button that nobody ever clicks because it's buried under seven layers of settings menus.
I navigate to the settings. There it is. "Request Data Audit." Gray text on a gray background. Designed to be invisible.
I look at Leo.
"I don't need to hack in."
He watches me. His tired eyes. His red-thread whites. The tendons in his hands, still frozen over the keyboard.
"I can walk in through the front door."
Leo's mouth opens. Closes. Opens again. "They'll see you. The moment you submit that request, they'll know what you're looking for."
"I know."
"They could accelerate the next deduction. They could hollow out something else. They could—"

"They can't." I say it flatly. Not because I'm brave. Bravery requires fear and I don't have fear anymore. I say it because it's structurally true. "They've already taken everything they can sell. Love. Trust. Empathy. Grief. Nostalgia. What's left? My annoyance? My boredom? Those aren't premium deductions. The remaining emotions are low-value inventory. They won't waste processing power on a revenge extraction."
Leo is quiet.

He's looking at me the way he looked at me last night, when I stepped out of his arms and said I couldn't feel it. Same expression. Same input. It doesn't land. But I see it. I catalog it. The geometry of his face, the angle of his jaw, the way his eyebrows pull together. He's afraid for me. That's a data point. Fear: Leo's current emotional state, directed at Chloe Hart. I can't feel it from the inside but I can see it from the outside, like reading an X-ray of someone else's broken bone.

"Seventy-one hours," I say. "The hearing is in seventy-one hours. If I submit the audit request now, they have to grant access within thirty business days — but the request itself is immediate. It's on record. And if they deny it or delay it, that's obstruction. Evelyn can use that."

"The audit gives us access to the gateway," I continue. "You monitor the data transfer during the audit. Map the server architecture in real time. Identify the enforcement protocol. Then write the blocking script targeted to the exact endpoint."
"You've thought this through," Leo says.
"I have nothing else to do with my brain."

It's not a joke. It's not bitter. It's a statement of fact. My brain is a processing engine running at full capacity with no emotional overhead. No anxiety loops. No second-guessing. No grief spirals consuming bandwidth. Every cycle is available for strategy. I am the most efficient version of myself. A scalpel.

That's the reversal they didn't see coming. Mephisto hollowed me out to make me compliant. Instead, they made me *precise*. The emotions that made me hesitate — the fear, the doubt, the paralyzing weight of caring about outcomes — those are gone. What's left is a person who can think in straight lines.
Evelyn was right. I don't know how to answer "how are you." That's the harm. But it's also the weapon.
I tap "Request Data Audit."
A confirmation dialog appears. *Are you sure you want to request a full audit of your personal data? This action cannot be undone.*
I tap confirm.
The screen goes white for a half-second. Then: *Your request has been submitted. Reference #HA-44917. You will receive access credentials within 30 business days.*
The Mephisto icon pulses once. Then goes still.
Leo exhales. I hear it — a slow release of breath, the kind that carries more data than any sensor could capture. Relief. Fear. Something in between.
I look at the phone. The screen. The reference number. HA-44917. Digits in a system. My name in their machine.

The ninety-second window is real. Leo found it. The glitch. The boot sequence. Every morning, for exactly ninety seconds, the original Chloe Hart is still in RAM — still capable, still *there* — before the system overwrites her with the flatline version. The emotions aren't deleted. They're locked. Archived behind a protocol that runs every morning and closes the door before I can walk through it.
But the door exists. I've seen the blueprints. And now I've filed a request to walk through the front gate.

Leo is watching me. I don't know what he sees. I don't know what my face is doing. Probably nothing. Probably the same flat expression I've been wearing since yesterday — the one that looks like calm but is actually absence.
I don't feel hope.

I *recognize* it. The same way I recognized love as a ghost pulse, the same way I identified Evelyn's personal question as an anomaly worth filing. Hope is a structure I can see even if I can't inhabit it. Architecture without occupancy. A room with no one inside, but the lights are on, and the door is unlocked, and somewhere in the building, there's a file with my name on it that contains everything they took.
Ninety seconds. Every morning. The original emotional architecture, still in RAM, waiting for the overwrite that comes like clockwork.
If we can stop the overwrite, the file plays.
The audit request is submitted. The hearing is in seventy-one hours. The glitch is mapped. The script is writable. The gateway has a front door.
I set the phone down. The ceramic mug sits next to it. VILLAGER ENERGY. The concert-A click still fading from the counter.
Ninety seconds.
That's all I need.
